key_insight: Ultrasound-driven cavitation bubbles can penetrate dense fibrin networks and, through repeated sub-fracture oscillatory loading, progressively soften and damage the mesh while creating transport pathways.
engineering_relevance: This provides a mechanistic basis for using acoustic cavitation to improve drug penetration and thrombolysis in stiff, fibrotic clots.
method: Drive microbubbles with ultrasound in dense fibrin networks
Track bubble penetration, oscillation, and 3D path formation
Measure network mechanics over thousands of loading cycles
evidence_signal: The abstract reports bubble-created 3D paths enabling nanobeads to infiltrate up to 200 µm deep, along with strong dissipation losses and softening over thousands of cycles.
watch_item: The bubble radial stress was insufficient to cause single-cycle fracture, so effectiveness depends on repeated loading and the resulting damage accumulation rather than immediate breakage.
paper_title: Interaction mechanics of acoustic cavitation with fibrin networks
